response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
When I was younger, my mind worked faster on these kinds of topics. Now, at eighty-eight years old, things are different. People come up with so many questions about the COVID vaccines that sometimes it leaves me feeling overwhelmed. I try to listen but then get tired easily because depression makes every day a challenge.

In Bologna where I live, my family and friends still ask what I think, especially since everyone is talking about this on their phones or computers now-a-days. Social media? Not really for me most of the time—only when they send me something interesting via text message from time to time.

I stick with things that are simple. Maybe if someone asks about vaccines, I can say how much science has helped us before, like my father who was an engineer used to explaining complex ideas simply back in his day. But sometimes these discussions go deep into the political mess or health scares without end; they’re too complicated for old brains these days.

I’d rather talk gardening than dive into debates about vaccines—though rock climbing keeps me active but not necessarily sharp either when it comes to long conversations on complex subjects like COVID protocols. It’s been a hard adjustment, and I often feel left behind.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: As an elderly retired man with low openness and conscientiousness scores from the Big Five personality traits, I find modern healthcare discussions overwhelming due to their complexity. My moderate agreeableness allows me to engage politely but not deeply without causing emotional strain (moderate neuroticism). Depression further complicates my ability to process information efficiently. I rely on simple analogies from past experiences with reliable technology, avoid social media which is too demanding for retired life, and prefer hobbies that ground me in the present rather than intellectual debates.
